title: Video Quiz: Foundations of Logistic Regression
section: 1.1 Q1 — What Does a Log Do?
text:
1.1 Q1 — What Does a Log Do?
The video says the core job of a logarithm is to isolate the exponent.

Complete the statement: “If \(2^5 = 32\), then \(\log_2(32) =\) ____ because…”
Verify in R: log(32, base = 2)
Why is this property useful when income values range from $7,000 to $455,000 in your dataset?



Code
# Your code here
log(32, base = 2)

title: Video Quiz: Foundations of Logistic Regression
text:
1.2 Q2 — Multiplication Becomes Addition
The video demonstrates: \(\log(a \times b) = \log(a) + \log(b)\).

Verify using a = 4, b = 8 (base 2) — compute both sides and confirm they are equal
In logistic regression, we multiply many small probabilities together. Explain in one sentence why the log property above makes this computationally safer.



Code
log(4 * 8, base = 2)
log(4, base = 2) + log(8, base = 2)

title: Video Quiz: Foundations of Logistic Regression
text:
1.3 Q3 — Geometric Mean in Marketing
The video shows the geometric mean is better than the arithmetic mean for data that multiplies rather than adds.
Five customers’ order values: $10, $20, $80, $40, $1,000.

Compute the arithmetic mean with mean()
Compute the geometric mean: exp(mean(log(x)))
Which better represents the “typical” customer — and why does the $1,000 order distort the arithmetic mean more?



Code
orders <- c(10, 20, 80, 40, 1000)
mean(orders)
exp(mean(log(orders)))

title: Video Quiz: Foundations of Logistic Regression
text:
1.4 Q4 — Log(0) and the Offset
The video explains \(\log(0)\) is undefined (negative infinity).

Run log(0) — what does R return?
Your Week 2 recipe uses step_log(..., offset = 1). Explain in one sentence why this offset is necessary.
What is log(0 + 1)? Why is adding 1 a safe lower bound?



Code
log(0)
log(0 + 1)

title: Video Quiz: Foundations of Logistic Regression
text:
1.5 Q5 — Base Conversion
The video notes that base 2, base 10, and natural log all follow the same rules and differ only by a constant factor.

For income = $49,021, compute log2(), log10(), and log() (natural)
Divide log() by log10() — what constant do you get?
After step_normalize() is applied in the recipe, does it matter which base was used for step_log()? Explain why or why not.



Code
income_val <- 49021
log(income_val, base = 2)
log10(income_val)
log(income_val)

# Ratio
log(income_val) / log10(income_val)

title: Video Quiz: Foundations of Logistic Regression
text:
2.1 Q6 — Probability vs. Odds
The video distinguishes probability from odds.
In mail_data, 142 customers responded (“yes”) out of 3,000 total.

Compute the probability of responding: \(P(\text{respond}) = \frac{\text{yes}}{\text{total}}\)
Compute the odds of responding: \(\text{Odds} = \frac{\text{yes}}{\text{no}} = \frac{P}{1 - P}\)
In plain English, what does an odds of 0.05 mean for a marketer trying to interpret campaign response?



Code
yes <- 142
no <- 3000 - 142

# Probability
p <- yes / (yes + no)
p

# Odds
odds <- yes / no
odds

# Or equivalently
p / (1 - p)

title: Video Quiz: Foundations of Logistic Regression
section: 2.2 Q7 — Converting Between Probability and Odds
text:
2.2 Q7 — Converting Between Probability and Odds
The video shows you can derive odds from probabilities using \(\text{Odds} = \frac{P}{1 - P}\).

If a customer has a 20% chance of responding, what are their odds?
If a customer has a 50% chance, what are their odds?
If a customer has an 80% chance, what are their odds?
What pattern do you notice? What happens to odds as probability approaches 1?



Code
prob_to_odds <- function(p) p / (1 - p)

prob_to_odds(0.20)
prob_to_odds(0.50)
prob_to_odds(0.80)

title: Video Quiz: Foundations of Logistic Regression
text:
2.3 Q8 — Why Log(Odds)?
The video explains that odds are asymmetric: odds against an event range 0 to 1, while odds in favor range 1 to infinity. Taking the log fixes this.

Compute log(odds) for probabilities 0.10, 0.50, and 0.90
Verify that log(odds) at \(P = 0.50\) equals exactly 0 — why does this make sense intuitively?
Are the log(odds) values symmetric around 0? What does this symmetry mean for modeling?



Code
probs <- c(0.10, 0.50, 0.90)
odds <- probs / (1 - probs)
log_odds <- log(odds)

tibble(probability = probs, odds = odds, log_odds = log_odds)

title: Video Quiz: Foundations of Logistic Regression
text:
2.4 Q9 — The Logit Function
The video introduces the logit as the log of the odds ratio, central to logistic regression.
\[\text{logit}(P) = \log\left(\frac{P}{1-P}\right)\]

Using mail_data, compute the overall response rate (probability)
Convert it to log(odds) using the formula above
In your Week 2 recipe, the model output .pred_yes is a probability. Write the R code to convert it to log(odds).



Code
# Overall response rate
p_respond <- mean(mail_data$responded == "yes")
p_respond

# Convert to log(odds)
log(p_respond / (1 - p_respond))

title: Video Quiz: Foundations of Logistic Regression
section: 2.5 Q10 — Log(Odds) and the Normal Distribution
text:
2.5 Q10 — Log(Odds) and the Normal Distribution
The video notes that log(odds) values often follow a normal distribution, making them useful for modeling binary outcomes.
Written question — no code required:

Your income variable was log-transformed in the recipe because raw income is right-skewed. The video suggests log(odds) of a binary outcome often follow a normal distribution. Why might this property make logistic regression a natural choice for modeling responded (yes/no)?
In 2–3 sentences, explain why working in log(odds) space is more mathematically convenient than working in probability space for a linear model.

title: Video Quiz: Foundations of Logistic Regression
section: 3.1 Q11 — What Is an Odds Ratio?
text:
3.1 Q11 — What Is an Odds Ratio?
The video defines an odds ratio (OR) as the ratio of two odds.
In mail_data, compare the odds of responding for Gold loyalty tier customers vs. Bronze loyalty tier customers.

Compute the odds of responding for Gold customers
Compute the odds of responding for Bronze customers
Compute the odds ratio: Gold odds ÷ Bronze odds
Is Gold tier a stronger or weaker predictor of response than Bronze?



Code
tier_summary <- mail_data |>
  group_by(loyalty_tier) |>
  summarise(
    yes = sum(responded == "yes"),
    no = sum(responded == "no"),
    odds = yes / no
  )

tier_summary

# Odds ratio: Gold vs Bronze
gold_odds <- tier_summary |> filter(loyalty_tier == "Gold") |> pull(odds)
bronze_odds <- tier_summary |> filter(loyalty_tier == "Bronze") |> pull(odds)

OR <- gold_odds / bronze_odds
OR

title: Video Quiz: Foundations of Logistic Regression
text:
3.2 Q12 — Asymmetry and Log(Odds Ratio)
The video explains that odds ratios are asymmetric: values below 1 (negative association) are compressed into 0–1, while values above 1 (positive association) range from 1 to infinity.

Compute log(OR) from Q11. Is it positive or negative?
Compute the odds ratio in the reverse direction: Bronze ÷ Gold. Then take log() of that. What do you notice?
Why is log(OR) = 0 the reference point for “no relationship”?



Code
# Log odds ratio
log(OR)

# Reverse direction
OR_reverse <- bronze_odds / gold_odds
log(OR_reverse)

title: Video Quiz: Foundations of Logistic Regression
text:
3.3 Q13 — Effect Size Interpretation
The video compares the odds ratio to R² as a measure of effect size — the larger the magnitude, the stronger the relationship.
Compute odds ratios for all four loyalty tiers against Bronze as the reference, then answer:

Which tier has the strongest association with response?
Which has the weakest?
How would you communicate the strongest result to a non-technical marketing manager in one sentence?



Code
tier_summary |>
  mutate(OR_vs_bronze = odds / bronze_odds, log_OR = log(OR_vs_bronze)) |>
  arrange(desc(abs(log_OR)))

title: Video Quiz: Foundations of Logistic Regression
text:
3.4 Q14 — Fisher’s Exact Test
The video introduces Fisher’s Exact Test for small samples to determine if an observed association is statistically significant.
Using Gold vs. Bronze customers from mail_data:

Build a 2×2 contingency table using table()
Run fisher.test() on it
What does the p-value tell you? Is the Gold/Bronze difference statistically significant at the 0.05 level?
When would you prefer Fisher’s Exact Test over a chi-square test?



Code
gold_bronze <- mail_data |>
  filter(loyalty_tier %in% c("Gold", "Bronze"))

ct <- table(gold_bronze$loyalty_tier, gold_bronze$responded)
ct

fisher.test(ct)

title: Video Quiz: Foundations of Logistic Regression
text:
3.5 Q15 — Chi-Square Test
The video explains the chi-square test compares observed values to expected values assuming no relationship exists.

Run chisq.test() on the same 2×2 table from Q14
Compare the p-value to Fisher’s Exact Test — are the conclusions the same?
The video notes chi-square works well for larger datasets. With 3,000 rows in mail_data, which test is more appropriate — and why?



Code
chisq.test(ct)

title: Video Quiz: Foundations of Logistic Regression
text:
3.6 Q16 — Wald Test Concept
The video explains the Wald Test evaluates how many standard deviations a log(odds ratio) is from zero, assuming a normal distribution.
Written question — no code required:

In your own words, what does it mean for a log(odds ratio) to be “many standard deviations from zero”?
The Wald test is used inside logistic regression to assess individual predictors. In the Week 2 notebook, you fitted a Lasso logistic regression. How does Lasso’s penalty relate to the Wald test’s goal of identifying which predictors are meaningfully different from zero?
The video says there is no single consensus on which test (Fisher, chi-square, Wald) is best. What practical guideline does it suggest?

title: Video Quiz: Foundations of Logistic Regression
section: 3.7 Q17 — Connecting Odds Ratios to the Course
text:
3.7 Q17 — Connecting Odds Ratios to the Course
Written question:
In logistic regression, the model coefficients are log(odds ratios) — each coefficient tells you how much the log(odds) of the outcome changes for a one-unit increase in a predictor.

In your Week 2 model, freq_12mo is a predictor. If its coefficient is positive, what does that imply about the odds of a customer responding as their purchase frequency increases?
After step_normalize(), predictors are on the same scale. Why does this make it easier to compare coefficients (and therefore odds ratios) across predictors with very different original units (e.g., income in dollars vs. frequency in counts)?

title: Video Quiz: Foundations of Logistic Regression
text:
4.1 Q18 — Linear vs. Logistic Regression
The video begins by reviewing linear regression, then explains why it fails for binary outcomes.

What does linear regression predict? What does logistic regression predict?
Why can’t you use a straight line to model a yes/no outcome like responded? What goes wrong at the extremes?
In mail_data, the response rate is about 4.7%. If you fitted a linear regression to predict responded (as 0/1), what value might it predict for a very frequent, high-income customer — and why is that problematic?

Written answer — no code required.

title: Video Quiz: Foundations of Logistic Regression
text:
4.2 Q19 — The S-Shaped Logistic Curve
The video shows that logistic regression fits an S-shaped curve (sigmoid function) that maps any input to a probability between 0 and 1.
\[P = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-(\beta_0 + \beta_1 x)}}\]

In R, plogis() computes this function. Evaluate it at inputs of −3, 0, and 3:



Code
plogis(-3) # very negative linear predictor
plogis(0) # at zero
plogis(3) # very positive linear predictor



What probability does plogis(0) return — and why does that make sense from the formula?
Notice that your dataset’s responded column was generated using plogis(...) — find that line in the setup chunk above and explain what the linear predictor inside plogis() represents in marketing terms.

title: Video Quiz: Foundations of Logistic Regression
section: 4.3 Q20 — Maximum Likelihood vs. Least Squares
text:
4.3 Q20 — Maximum Likelihood vs. Least Squares
The video explains a key difference in how the two models are fitted:

Linear regression: minimizes the sum of squared residuals (least squares)
Logistic regression: maximizes the likelihood of the observed data

Written question:

Why can’t logistic regression use least squares? (Hint: what is the “residual” when the outcome is 0 or 1?)
In plain English, what does it mean for logistic regression to find the curve that “maximizes the likelihood” of the observed responses?
The video mentions that logistic regression does not have the same concept of a residual. How does this affect how we assess model fit — and what metrics do we use instead? (Reference your Week 3 notebook.)

Video Quiz: Foundations of Logistic Regression
section: 4.5 Q22 — Wald’s Test and Variable Selection

title: Video Quiz: Foundations of Logistic Regression
section: 4.6 Q23 — Synthesis: From Logs to Logistic Regression
text:
4.6 Q23 — Synthesis: From Logs to Logistic Regression
This final question ties all four videos together.
Written question (3–5 sentences):
The four videos form a conceptual chain: Logs → Odds → Odds Ratios → Logistic Regression.

Explain in your own words how each step connects to the next:

Why do we take the log of odds (not just use odds directly)?
Why do we compare odds as a ratio rather than a difference?
How does the logit (log odds) become the linear predictor inside logistic regression?

In the context of the direct-mail campaign in mail_data, describe in one sentence what the logistic regression model is actually computing when it produces .pred_yes = 0.08 for a specific customer.

title: Data Splitting and Preprocessing with Recipes
text:
On this page
   
  
  1 Setup
  2 The Data Set
  3 Data Splitting
  4 Build a Recipe
  


1 Setup


Code
library(tidymodels)
library(tidyverse)
library(janitor)
library(skimr)

tidymodels_prefer()




2 The Data Set


Code
# ── Synthetic direct-mail dataset ──────────────────────────────────────────────
set.seed(123)
n <- 3000

mail_data <- tibble(
  customer_id = paste0("C", str_pad(1:n, 5, pad = "0")),
  age = round(rnorm(n, mean = 45, sd = 12)),
  income = round(rlnorm(n, meanlog = 10.8, sdlog = 0.6)), # right-skewed
  recency_days = round(rexp(n, rate = 1 / 60)), # days since last purchase
  freq_12mo = rpois(n, lambda = 3), # purchases in 12 months
  avg_order_amt = round(rlnorm(n, meanlog = 4.2, sdlog = 0.5), 2),
  channel = sample(
    c("email", "direct_mail", "digital"),
    n,
    replace = TRUE,
    prob = c(0.5, 0.3, 0.2)
  ),
  region = sample(
    c("West", "South", "Midwest", "Northeast"),
    n,
    replace = TRUE
  ),
  loyalty_tier = sample(
    c("Bronze", "Silver", "Gold", "Platinum"),
    n,
    replace = TRUE,
    prob = c(0.4, 0.3, 0.2, 0.1)
  ),
  responded = rbinom(
    n,
    1,
    prob = plogis(
      -3 +
        0.02 * (age - 45) +
        0.3 * log(income / 50000) +
        0.1 * freq_12mo -
        0.005 * recency_days
    )
  )
) |>
  mutate(
    income = if_else(runif(n) < 0.08, NA_real_, income),
    avg_order_amt = if_else(runif(n) < 0.05, NA_real_, avg_order_amt),
    responded = factor(responded, levels = c(1, 0), labels = c("yes", "no"))
  )
# introduce ~8 % missingness in income and avg_order_amt

glimpse(mail_data)

title: Week 2: Data Splitting and Preprocessing with recipes
section: 2.1 Explaining how Income distribution changes from normal to logged data
text:
2.1 Explaining how Income distribution changes from normal to logged data


Code
library(tidyverse)
library(patchwork)

set.seed(42)
n <- 5000
meanlog <- 10.8
sdlog <- 0.6

income <- rlnorm(n, meanlog = meanlog, sdlog = sdlog)
log_income <- log(income)

median_raw <- exp(meanlog)
mean_raw <- exp(meanlog + sdlog^2 / 2)
gsd_low <- median_raw / exp(sdlog)
gsd_high <- median_raw * exp(sdlog)
log_median <- meanlog
log_mean <- meanlog + sdlog^2 / 2
log_sd_low <- meanlog - sdlog
log_sd_high <- meanlog + sdlog

df_raw <- tibble(income = income) |> filter(income < 250000)
df_log <- tibble(log_income = log_income)

p1 <- ggplot(df_raw, aes(x = income)) +
  annotate(
    "rect",
    xmin = gsd_low,
    xmax = gsd_high,
    ymin = -Inf,
    ymax = Inf,
    fill = "#97C459",
    alpha = 0.15
  ) +
  geom_histogram(
    bins = 50,
    fill = "#85B7EB",
    color = "#378ADD",
    linewidth = 0.3
  ) +
  geom_vline(aes(xintercept = median_raw, color = "Median"), linewidth = 1) +
  geom_vline(
    aes(xintercept = mean_raw, color = "Mean"),
    linewidth = 1,
    linetype = "dashed"
  ) +
  annotate(
    "text",
    x = median_raw - 1500,
    y = Inf,
    label = "median\n$49K",
    color = "#E24B4A",
    vjust = 1.3,
    hjust = 1,
    size = 3
  ) +
  annotate(
    "text",
    x = mean_raw + 1500,
    y = Inf,
    label = "mean\n$59K",
    color = "#BA7517",
    vjust = 1.3,
    hjust = 0,
    size = 3
  ) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Median" = "#E24B4A", "Mean" = "#BA7517")) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    labels = scales::dollar_format(scale = 1e-3, suffix = "K")
  ) +
  labs(x = "Income ($)", y = "Count", title = "Income (original scale)") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    legend.position = "none",
    plot.title = element_text(size = 11, face = "bold", color = "gray40")
  )

p2 <- ggplot(df_log, aes(x = log_income)) +
  annotate(
    "rect",
    xmin = log_sd_low,
    xmax = log_sd_high,
    ymin = -Inf,
    ymax = Inf,
    fill = "#97C459",
    alpha = 0.15
  ) +
  geom_histogram(
    bins = 40,
    fill = "#85B7EB",
    color = "#378ADD",
    linewidth = 0.3
  ) +
  geom_vline(aes(xintercept = log_median, color = "Median"), linewidth = 1) +
  geom_vline(
    aes(xintercept = log_mean, color = "Mean"),
    linewidth = 1,
    linetype = "dashed"
  ) +
  annotate(
    "text",
    x = log_median - 0.03,
    y = Inf,
    label = "median\n10.8",
    color = "#E24B4A",
    vjust = 1.3,
    hjust = 1,
    size = 3
  ) +
  annotate(
    "text",
    x = log_mean + 0.03,
    y = Inf,
    label = "mean\n10.98",
    color = "#BA7517",
    vjust = 1.3,
    hjust = 0,
    size = 3
  ) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Median" = "#E24B4A", "Mean" = "#BA7517")) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(8, 14, by = 0.5)) +
  labs(
    x = "log(income)",
    y = "Count",
    title = "log(income) — transformed scale"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    legend.position = "none",
    plot.title = element_text(size = 11, face = "bold", color = "gray40")
  )

p1 / p2















NoteLog-Normal Parameters: What Do They Mean?



The income variable was generated using a log-normal distribution via rlnorm(n, meanlog = 10.8, sdlog = 0.6). The parameters meanlog and sdlog describe the distribution of log(income) — not income itself. To recover interpretable statistics on the original dollar scale:
Median income (the “typical” earner): \[\text{Median} = e^{\mu} = e^{10.8} \approx \$49{,}021\]
Mean income (pulled up by high earners): \[\text{Mean} = e^{\mu + \sigma^2/2} = e^{10.8 + 0.18} \approx \$58{,}689\]
Why are they different? Income is right-skewed — a small number of high earners pull the mean above the median. The median is preserved under exponentiation (the middle person stays the middle person), but the mean is not. The correction term \(e^{\sigma^2/2}\) accounts for the skew introduced by the long right tail.
Standard deviation on the dollar scale is less commonly reported for log-normal data. Instead, practitioners often describe spread using the geometric standard deviation (GSD): \[\text{GSD} = e^{\sigma} = e^{0.6} \approx 1.82\]
This means a typical income is within a factor of 1.82 above or below the median — roughly between \(\$49{,}021 / 1.82 \approx \$26{,}935\) and \(\$49{,}021 \times 1.82 \approx \$89{,}218\).

title: Week 2: Data Splitting and Preprocessing with recipes
text:
3.1 Why We Split
The test set simulates unseen future customers. Any transformation parameter (mean, standard deviation, imputation value) estimated on the full dataset “leaks” information about the test set into the model — this is called data leakage and leads to overly optimistic performance estimates.
The golden rule: fit everything on training data, apply to test data.

4.2 Deep Dive: Each Step

4.2.1 Imputation (step_impute_median)


Code

mail_train |>

  pivot_longer(

Important



Why median and not mean? The median is robust to the same right-skew that makes these variables candidates for log transformation. Using the mean on a skewed distribution would impute an “average” that may not represent the typical customer.




4.2.2 Log Transformation (step_log)


Code
# Visual: income before and after log10
p1 <- mail_train |>
  filter(!is.na(income)) |>
  ggplot(aes(income)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 40, fill = "steelblue", alpha = 0.7) +
  labs(title = "Income — raw", x = "income ($)")

  filter(!is.na(income)) |>

4.2.3 Normalization (step_normalize)
Centering and scaling puts all numeric variables on the same unit-free scale. This is required for distance-based models (KNN, SVM) and strongly recommended for logistic regression with regularization.

  data = mail_train
) |>
  step_impute_median(all_numeric_predictors()) |>
  step_log(income, base = 10, offset = 1) |>
  step_normalize(all_numeric_predictors())

title: Week 2: Data Splitting and Preprocessing with recipes
text:
6.1 Regularization Concept






NoteRegularization: Ridge, Lasso, and Elastic Net




Why Regularization?
Ordinary logistic regression finds coefficients that best fit the training data. But with many predictors — common in marketing datasets with demographics, behavioral variables, and RFM features — the model can overfit: it learns the noise in the training data rather than the true signal, and performs poorly on new customers.
Regularization fixes this by adding a penalty term to the loss function that discourages large coefficients. The model is forced to be simpler, which usually means it generalizes better to new data.
The penalized loss function looks like this:
\[\text{Loss} = \text{(fit to training data)} + \lambda \cdot \text{(penalty on coefficients)}\]
The \(\lambda\) (penalty) parameter controls how hard we squeeze the coefficients:

\(\lambda = 0\): no regularization, ordinary logistic regression
Large \(\lambda\): heavy shrinkage, very simple model




Three Flavors of Regularization

Ridge (L2)
Ridge adds the sum of squared coefficients as the penalty:
\[\text{Penalty} = \sum_{j} \beta_j^2\]
Ridge shrinks all coefficients toward zero but never sets them exactly to zero. Every predictor stays in the model, just with a smaller influence. This works well when you believe most predictors are genuinely useful and want to reduce their magnitude without discarding any.
In tidymodels: mixture = 0


Lasso (L1)
Lasso adds the sum of absolute coefficients as the penalty:
\[\text{Penalty} = \sum_{j} |\beta_j|\]
Lasso can shrink coefficients all the way to exactly zero, effectively removing those predictors from the model. This makes Lasso a built-in variable selection tool — it produces a sparse model that only keeps the most important predictors. In a direct-mail context, this is useful when you have dozens of customer attributes and want the model to tell you which ones actually drive response.
In tidymodels: mixture = 1


Elastic Net
Elastic net blends Ridge and Lasso:
\[\text{Penalty} = \alpha \sum_{j} |\beta_j| + (1 - \alpha) \sum_{j} \beta_j^2\]
The mixture parameter (\(\alpha\)) controls the blend:



mixture
Behavior




1
Pure Lasso


0
Pure Ridge


0.5
Equal blend of both



Elastic net is often the safest default when you are unsure: it can do variable selection like Lasso while handling correlated predictors more stably (Lasso tends to arbitrarily pick one variable from a correlated group; elastic net can keep both with reduced coefficients).
In tidymodels: 0 < mixture < 1




Choosing \(\lambda\)
The penalty value (\(\lambda\)) is a hyperparameter — it is not learned from the data during model fitting. Instead, it must be chosen beforehand or tuned using cross-validation. A common workflow in tidymodels is to replace the fixed value with tune() and search over a grid of candidates:
lr_spec <- logistic_reg(penalty = tune(), mixture = 1) |>
  set_engine("glmnet") |>
  set_mode("classification")
For now, we use penalty = 0.01 as a reasonable starting value. Later in the course we will tune this properly.



Quick Reference









Method
Penalty type
Zeroes out predictors?
Best when…




Ridge
Sum of squares
No
Most predictors are useful


Lasso
Sum of absolutes
Yes
Many irrelevant predictors


Elastic net

Sometimes
Unsure, or predictors are correlated

title: Teacher’s Notes: Week 2 Log Assignments (L.1–L.5)
text:
1.1 Answer Key


Code
# Natural log
log(233070)


[1] 12.35909


Code
# Log base 10
log10(233070)


[1] 5.367486


Code
# equivalently:
log(233070, base = 10)


[1] 5.367486


Expected answers:

Natural log: ≈ 12.36
Log base 10: ≈ 5.37

Written answer (Q4): Either base 10 or natural log is acceptable for the recipe — they differ only by a constant factor (log(10) ≈ 2.303), and step_normalize() absorbs that difference. The base = 10 choice is a convention for interpretability: log10 values map intuitively to order of magnitude ($10K → 4, $100K → 5, $1M → 6).

title: Teacher’s Notes: Week 2 Log Assignments (L.1–L.5)
text:
1.2 What to Look For

Both numeric answers correct ✓
Student recognizes the two results are proportional, not equal ✓
Written justification mentions either interpretability or that step_normalize() makes the base choice irrelevant for modeling ✓

title: Teacher’s Notes: Week 2 Log Assignments (L.1–L.5)
text:
1.3 Common Mistakes

Confusing log() (natural) with log10() — watch for students who report the same number for both
Claiming base 10 is “more accurate” — both bases are equally valid; correctness is about convention and interpretability

title: Teacher’s Notes: Week 2 Log Assignments (L.1–L.5)
text:
2.1 Answer Key


Code
# Median income
exp(10.8)


[1] 49020.8


Code
# Mean income — note the correction term sigma^2 / 2
mu    <- 10.8
sigma <- 0.6
exp(mu + sigma^2 / 2)


[1] 58688.55


Code
# Verify the formula directly
exp(10.8 + 0.6^2 / 2)


[1] 58688.55


Expected answers:

Median: ≈ $49,021
Mean: ≈ $58,689 (some rounding acceptable: $58,670–$58,710)

Written answer (Q4): Income is right-skewed — a small number of very high earners pull the arithmetic mean upward. The median is the middle value and is preserved under the exp() transformation. The correction term \(e^{\sigma^2/2}\) accounts for the asymmetric “stretching” that exponentiation applies to a symmetric log-scale distribution.

title: Teacher’s Notes: Week 2 Log Assignments (L.1–L.5)
text:
2.2 What to Look For

Students must use exp(), not 10^x — both are valid mathematically (exp(10.8) = 10^(10.8/log(10))), but exp() is the correct inverse of the natural log used in rlnorm()
The correction term sigma^2 / 2 = 0.18 must be added before exp(), not multiplied after
Written explanation should mention skewness or outliers pulling the mean up

title: Teacher’s Notes: Week 2 Log Assignments (L.1–L.5)
text:
2.3 Common Mistakes

Using 10^10.8 instead of exp(10.8) — gives a different and incorrect answer (~630 billion) because the log base used in rlnorm() is natural
Forgetting the sigma^2/2 correction for the mean
Claiming mean = median for income — the whole point is they differ

title: Teacher’s Notes: Week 2 Log Assignments (L.1–L.5)
text:
2.4 Discussion Point
Ask: “If a new customer has unknown income, which value would you use for imputation — the mean or median from the log scale — and why?” This connects directly to Exercise 8.1 in the main notebook (median vs. mean imputation).

title: Teacher’s Notes: Week 2 Log Assignments (L.1–L.5)
text:
3.1 Answer Key


Code
# GSD
gsd <- exp(0.6)
gsd  # ≈ 1.822


[1] 1.822119


Code
# Median income (from L.2)
median_income <- exp(10.8)  # ≈ 49,021

# Typical range
lower <- median_income / gsd
upper <- median_income * gsd

cat("GSD           :", round(gsd, 3), "\n")


GSD           : 1.822 


Code
cat("Median income : $", round(median_income), "\n")


Median income : $ 49021 


Code
cat("Lower bound   : $", round(lower), "\n")


Lower bound   : $ 26903 


Code
cat("Upper bound   : $", round(upper), "\n")


Upper bound   : $ 89322 


Expected answers:

GSD: ≈ 1.822
Lower: ≈ $26,908 (acceptable range: $26,800–$27,100)
Upper: ≈ $89,301 (acceptable range: $89,000–$89,500)

These should match the green shaded band in the Week 2 notebook plot.
Written answer (Q4): A typical customer’s income falls between roughly $27K and $89K. Unlike the regular standard deviation (which adds and subtracts), the GSD multiplies and divides, reflecting that income variation is proportional, not additive. A marketer might use this to define a “mainstream income segment” for targeting.

text:

GSD computed as exp(sdlog), not exp(meanlog) — common confusion
Range uses multiplication/division, not addition/subtraction ✓
Values roughly match the notebook’s green band ✓
Written interpretation is in plain English (dollars, not log units) ✓

title: Teacher’s Notes: Week 2 Log Assignments (L.1–L.5)
text:
3.3 Common Mistakes

Using exp(meanlog) instead of exp(sdlog) for GSD
Adding/subtracting GSD instead of multiplying/dividing: 49021 - 1.822 vs. 49021 / 1.822 — the subtraction is meaningless

4.1 Answer Key

mail_data |>
  summarise(
    median_income = median(income, na.rm = TRUE),
    mean_income   = mean(income,   na.rm = TRUE)

Code
# Step 2: create log_income, compute its median and mean
mail_data |>
  mutate(log_income = log(income)) |>
  summarise(
    median_log = median(log_income, na.rm = TRUE),
    mean_log   = mean(log_income,   na.rm = TRUE)
  )



  



Code
# Step 3: back-transform
log_stats <- mail_data |>
  mutate(log_income = log(income)) |>
  summarise(
    median_log = median(log_income, na.rm = TRUE),
    mean_log   = mean(log_income,   na.rm = TRUE)
  )

cat("exp(median log_income):", round(exp(log_stats$median_log)), "\n")


exp(median log_income): 49331 


Code
cat("exp(mean log_income)  :", round(exp(log_stats$mean_log)),   "\n")


exp(mean log_income)  : 48916 


Code
cat("Theoretical median    :", round(exp(10.8)),                  "\n")


Theoretical median    : 49021 


Code
cat("Theoretical mean      :", round(exp(10.8 + 0.6^2/2)),       "\n")


Theoretical mean      : 58689 


Expected observations:

Empirical median of income ≈ $49,332 (close to theoretical $49,021)
Empirical mean of income ≈ $58,350 (close to theoretical $58,689)
Small differences are expected due to finite sampling (n = 3,000) and the 8% missingness in income
exp(median(log_income)) recovers a value close to the theoretical median — the geometric mean of income
exp(mean(log_income)) is close to but not exactly the arithmetic mean of income — it is the geometric mean on the log scale

This exercise plants a seed for Week 3’s resampling discussion: “A single sample of 3,000 gives slightly different statistics than the population parameters. What does that imply about how much we should trust a model trained on one particular sample?”

result <- tibble(
  income = c(10000, 100000, 1000000)

  mutate(
    log_natural = log(income),
    log_base10  = log10(income)

result

# Step 3: compute the ratio

  mutate(ratio = log_natural / log_base10)

log(10)


[1] 2.302585

income

The ratio is always log(10) ≈ 2.303.
Written answer (Q4): Since natural log = log10 × log(10), the two columns are related by a constant multiplier. step_normalize() centers by the mean and divides by the standard deviation — both operations absorb any constant multiplier, leaving the normalized values identical regardless of which base was used. So the choice of base is a cosmetic preference, not a modeling decision.

title: Teacher’s Notes: Week 2 Log Assignments (L.1–L.5)

5.2 What to Look For

Written explanation correctly invokes step_normalize() as the reason base doesn’t matter ✓
Log base 10 values are exact integers (4, 5, 6) — a nice sanity check ✓

Dividing in the wrong direction (log10 / log_natural) — gives 1/2.303 ≈ 0.434 = log10(e), which is also correct but less intuitive
Claiming base 10 is “better” without referencing normalization
Not noticing that log10 of powers of 10 are exact integers

title: Week 2 Exercises — Teacher’s Notes
text:
1.2 What to Look For

Records moved: 300 rows shift from training to test (from 2,400/600 to 2,100/900)
Response rate: Should stay approximately ~4.7% in both sets — that’s exactly what strata = responded guarantees. If students see large differences, they may have removed the strata argument by mistake.
Common mistake: Students may confuse prop (training proportion) with test proportion. A good follow-up question: “If prop = 0.70, what fraction is the test set?”

title: Week 2 Exercises — Teacher’s Notes
text:
1.3 Discussion Point

A 70/30 split gives a larger test set — useful for getting more stable performance estimates, but at the cost of a smaller training set.
With only 3,000 rows and a 4.7% response rate, the training set contains only ~98 responders at 70/30 — already quite few for a classification model.



Code
train_70 |> count(responded)



  



Code
0.0433 * 2100


[1] 90.93

title: Week 2 Exercises — Teacher’s Notes
Week 2 Exercises — Teacher’s Notes
text:
2.2 What to Look For

Mean income imputation will be noticeably higher than median (~$58K vs ~$49K) because of right-skew. The few very high earners drag the mean up.
Mean imputation assigns a value that is above the 50th percentile — most customers with missing income would be assumed to earn more than half the observed customers. That’s not a neutral assumption.
Preferred: median, because it is robust to the right-skew in income and avg_order_amt.

title: Week 2 Exercises — Teacher’s Notes
text:
2.3 Discussion Point
This is a good opportunity to reinforce that imputation choice is a design decision with business implications — imputing a higher income could inflate predicted response probabilities for customers whose income we don’t know. Median is the safer, more conservative choice.

3.1 Answer Key


Code
# loyalty_tier must be an ordered factor first
mail_data_ord <- mail_data |>
  mutate(
    loyalty_tier = factor(
      loyalty_tier,
      levels = c("Bronze", "Silver", "Gold", "Platinum"),
      ordered = TRUE
    )

mail_train_ord <- mail_data_ord |> slice(mail_split$in_id)

rec_ordinal <- recipe(responded ~ ., data = mail_train_ord) |>
  update_role(customer_id, new_role = "ID") |>
  step_impute_median(all_numeric_predictors()) |>
  step_log(income, avg_order_amt, recency_days, base = 10, offset = 1) |>
  step_normalize(all_numeric_predictors()) |>
  step_ordinalscore(loyalty_tier) |> # ordinal score for tier
  step_dummy(all_nominal_predictors()) |> # dummy for channel + region
  step_zv(all_predictors())

prep_ord <- prep(rec_ordinal)
baked_orig <- bake(mail_prep, new_data = NULL)
baked_ord <- bake(prep_ord, new_data = NULL)

cat("Original columns (dummy encoding):  ", ncol(baked_orig), "\n")


Original columns (dummy encoding):   15

Ordinal encoding columns:            13

baked_ord |> select(starts_with("loyalty")) |> head()

title: Week 2 Exercises — Teacher’s Notes
text:
3.2 What to Look For

Column count drops by 2:

dummy encoding creates 3 columns for loyalty_tier (k − 1 = 4 − 1 = 3);
ordinal scoring creates just 1 integer column (Bronze=1, Silver=2, Gold=3, Platinum=4).

Watch for the error:

if students run step_ordinalscore() without first converting loyalty_tier to an ordered factor, R will throw: “Ordinal factor variables should be selected as inputs into this step.” The fix is to add mutate() or step_mutate() before step_ordinalscore().

Key teaching point:

ordinal encoding assumes equal spacing between tiers

Bronze→Silver = Silver→Gold = Gold→Platinum.

That may not be true. Custom spacing can be applied via the convert argument.

title: Week 2 Exercises — Teacher’s Notes
Week 2 Exercises — Teacher’s Notes
text:
4.2 What to Look For

A new column freq_12mo_x_income should appear in the baked data.
Step order matters: step_interact() must come after step_normalize()

students who put it before normalization will get the interaction of unstandardized variables, which can lead to scaling issues.

Marketing intuition: high purchase frequency + high income may have a multiplicative effect on response — a very frequent, affluent customer is not just the sum of “frequent” and “rich,” they may be disproportionately likely to respond. Conversely, a high-frequency, low-income customer might be budget-sensitive, dampening the frequency effect.

title: Week 2 Exercises — Teacher’s Notes
text:
4.3 Discussion Point
Interaction terms increase model complexity and can hurt regularized models if not needed. A good follow-up: “After adding this interaction, does roc_auc improve? If not, should we keep it?”

title: Week 2 Exercises — Teacher’s Notes
Week 2 Exercises — Teacher’s Notes
text:
5.2 What to Look For

Performance typically drops noticeably without normalization for Lasso, because the penalty is applied equally to all coefficients in their raw units. Without normalization, predictors on larger scales (income in tens of thousands of dollars) get penalized proportionally more than predictors on smaller scales (freq_12mo, which ranges 0–10), distorting variable selection.
Students may be surprised that log transformation alone isn’t enough — log(income) is still on a very different scale than freq_12mo.

title: Week 2 Exercises — Teacher’s Notes
text:
5.3 Discussion Point

This exercise makes the practical consequence of normalization visible rather than just stating it as a rule.
A useful follow-up: “For a tree-based model like random forest, would removing normalization matter?” (Answer: no — trees split on rank order, not magnitude.)

title: Week 2 Exercises — Teacher’s Notes
Week 2 Exercises — Teacher’s Notes
text:
6.2 What to Look For

The standard deviation of response rate across splits should be notably smaller with stratification (~0.0 with strata, vs potentially 0.3–0.8% without).
With only 142 responders in 3,000 rows, a 600-row test set contains only ~28 expected responders. Random variation in that small count creates meaningful swings in response rate without stratification.

title: Week 2 Exercises — Teacher’s Notes
text:
6.3 Discussion Point
This exercise provides empirical evidence for something students may have accepted on faith. The fact that stratification nearly eliminates response rate variance across splits is a concrete, memorable result.

title: Week 2 Exercises — Teacher’s Notes
Week 2 Exercises — Teacher’s Notes
text:
7.2 What to Look For

The mean and standard deviation of income will differ slightly between the two preps, because the training set (2,400 rows) and test set (600 rows) are different samples with slightly different statistics.
Even small differences matter: if test-set statistics are used to normalize, then the model has implicitly “seen” the test set during preprocessing — making performance estimates optimistic.
Key point: this kind of leakage is insidious because the code runs without errors and the performance difference is usually small — but it’s conceptually wrong and can mislead model selection.

title: Week 2 Exercises — Teacher’s Notes
text:
7.3 Discussion Point
“Would this matter more with a small or large dataset, and why?” (Answer: more with small data, where training and test statistics diverge more.)

title: Week 2 Exercises — Teacher’s Notes
text:
8.2 What to Look For

Step 1 (imputation): median values for income (~$49K on raw scale, before log) and avg_order_amt (~$66).

These should match median(mail_train$income, na.rm = TRUE) and median(mail_train$avg_order_amt, na.rm = TRUE).

Step 3 (normalization):


8.2.0.1 What step_normalize() does — Z-score standardization
\[
z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}
\]
Where:

x = the original value
μ = the mean of that variable (computed from training data during prep())
σ = the standard deviation of that variable (computed from training data during prep())
z = the resulting standardized value
means and standard deviations — after log transformation, so these are on the log scale. income mean ≈ 10.8, sd ≈ 0.6 (matching meanlog / sdlog from the data generator, which is a nice sanity check).
Once normalzied, the data should have mean = 0 with sd = 1.



Code
bake(mail_prep, new_data = NULL) |>
  summarize(mean = mean(income), sd = sd(income))



  




Students should notice that number = 3 refers to the third step in the recipe, not a fixed step type — if they reorder steps, the numbering changes.

title: Week 2 Exercises — Teacher’s Notes
text:
9.2 What to Look For

Without update_role(), customer_id is treated as a regular predictor. Because it is a character variable, step_dummy() will try to create dummy variables from it — 3,000 unique IDs means up to 2,999 dummy columns, most of which step_zv() will then remove as zero-variance columns.
The column count will balloon dramatically before step_zv() cleans it up — worth showing students what happens before that safety net is applied.
Why ID variables must not be predictors: each customer ID is unique, so the model could perfectly memorize training labels by ID. It would score 100% on training data and near-random on test data — classic overfitting with a degenerate cause.

title: Week 2 Exercises — Teacher’s Notes
text:
9.3 Discussion Point
This is a memorable example of a silent error — the code runs fine, the model fits, but the results are meaningless. Ask students: “How would you detect this in practice without looking at the code?” (Answer: suspiciously high training accuracy, near-chance test accuracy.)

title: Teacher’s Notes: Video Quiz — Foundations of Logistic Regression (Q1–Q23)
section: 1.1 Q1 — What Does a Log Do?
text:
1.1 Q1 — What Does a Log Do?


Code
log(32, base = 2)


[1] 5


Expected answer: 5
Complete statement: “If \(2^5 = 32\), then \(\log_2(32) = 5\) because the logarithm tells us the exponent we need to raise 2 to in order to get 32.”
Q3 written answer: Income ranges from ~$7K to ~$455K — a ratio of about 65×. On the raw scale that spread makes linear modeling difficult. Log compresses it to a much narrower range (~3.8 to ~5.7 in log10), making the relationship between income and response more linear.
Common mistake: Answering “5” without explaining why that’s what a log does — push for the “isolates the exponent” framing from the video.

title: Teacher’s Notes: Video Quiz — Foundations of Logistic Regression (Q1–Q23)
text:
1.2 Q2 — Multiplication Becomes Addition


Code
log(4 * 8, base = 2)


[1] 5


Code
log(4, base = 2) + log(8, base = 2)


[1] 5


Expected answer: Both equal 5 — confirming the rule.
Written answer (Q2): When multiplying many small probabilities (e.g., likelihoods), the product can underflow to zero in floating-point arithmetic. Log converts multiplication to addition, keeping the numbers in a stable numeric range — this is why the log-likelihood is maximized instead of the likelihood directly.
Common mistake: Students who just report the numbers without explaining the computational benefit should be pushed to connect it to likelihood in logistic regression.

title: Teacher’s Notes: Video Quiz — Foundations of Logistic Regression (Q1–Q23)
text:
1.3 Q3 — Geometric Mean in Marketing


Code
orders <- c(10, 20, 80, 40, 1000)
mean(orders)


[1] 230


Code
exp(mean(log(orders)))


[1] 57.708


Expected answers:

Arithmetic mean: 230
Geometric mean: ≈ 56.2

Written answer: $56 is a much more representative “typical” order value than $230, which is inflated by the single $1,000 order. The arithmetic mean squares the deviation of that outlier (via the averaging process), while the geometric mean works in log-space where the outlier is less extreme.
What to look for: Student should explicitly name which is more representative and why the outlier affects the arithmetic mean more.

title: Teacher’s Notes: Video Quiz — Foundations of Logistic Regression (Q1–Q23)
text:
1.4 Q4 — Log(0) and the Offset


Code
log(0)


[1] -Inf


Code
log(0 + 1)


[1] 0


Expected answers:

log(0) returns -Inf
log(0 + 1) = log(1) = 0

Written answer (Q2): The offset ensures that even a customer with zero recency days or zero order amount doesn’t produce -Inf after log transformation, which would crash the model.
Written answer (Q3): log(1) = 0 because any base raised to the power 0 equals 1. Adding 1 as a lower bound means the minimum possible log-transformed value is 0, not negative infinity — a safe, neutral floor.

title: Teacher’s Notes: Video Quiz — Foundations of Logistic Regression (Q1–Q23)
text:
1.5 Q5 — Base Conversion


Code
income_val <- 49021
log(income_val, base = 2)


[1] 15.58111


Code
log10(income_val)


[1] 4.690382


Code
log(income_val)


[1] 10.8


Code
log(income_val) / log10(income_val)


[1] 2.302585


Expected answers:

Base 2: ≈ 15.58
Base 10: ≈ 4.69
Natural: ≈ 10.80
Ratio: ≈ 2.303 = log(10)

Written answer (Q3): The ratio is the constant log(10) ≈ 2.303 — any value in natural log equals its log10 value × 2.303. Since step_normalize() subtracts the mean and divides by the standard deviation, multiplying by a constant changes both the mean and SD proportionally, and the resulting z-scores are identical regardless of base.
What to look for: Student must invoke step_normalize() explicitly, not just state “it doesn’t matter.”

title: Teacher’s Notes: Video Quiz — Foundations of Logistic Regression (Q1–Q23)
text:
2.1 Q6 — Probability vs. Odds


Code
yes <- 142
no <- 3000 - 142

p <- yes / (yes + no)
odds <- yes / no

cat("Probability:", round(p, 4), "\n")


Probability: 0.0473 


Code
cat("Odds       :", round(odds, 4), "\n")


Odds       : 0.0497 


Expected answers:

Probability: ≈ 0.0473 (4.73%)
Odds: ≈ 0.0497

Written answer (Q3): Odds of 0.05 means for every customer who responds, about 20 do not. A marketer might say: “We expect roughly 1 responder for every 20 non-responders mailed.”
Common mistake: Confusing probability (0.0473) and odds (0.0497) — they are close when the event is rare but diverge substantially as probability increases. Worth demonstrating with Q7.

title: Teacher’s Notes: Video Quiz — Foundations of Logistic Regression (Q1–Q23)
section: 2.2 Q7 — Converting Between Probability and Odds
text:
2.2 Q7 — Converting Between Probability and Odds


Code
prob_to_odds <- function(p) p / (1 - p)
prob_to_odds(0.20)


[1] 0.25


Code
prob_to_odds(0.50)


[1] 1


Code
prob_to_odds(0.80)


[1] 4


Expected answers:

P = 0.20 → odds = 0.25 (1 in 4 chance: 1 success per 3 failures)
P = 0.50 → odds = 1.00 (even odds: 1 success per 1 failure)
P = 0.80 → odds = 4.00 (4 successes per 1 failure)

Pattern (Q4): As probability approaches 1, odds grow without bound toward infinity. The relationship is nonlinear and asymmetric — which is exactly the problem log(odds) fixes.

title: Teacher’s Notes: Video Quiz — Foundations of Logistic Regression (Q1–Q23)
text:
2.3 Q8 — Why Log(Odds)?


Code
probs <- c(0.10, 0.50, 0.90)
odds <- probs / (1 - probs)
log_odds <- log(odds)

tibble(probability = probs, odds = odds, log_odds = log_odds)



  



Expected answers:



probability
odds
log_odds




0.10
0.111
−2.197


0.50
1.000
0.000


0.90
9.000
2.197



Q2: log(odds) at P = 0.50 is 0 because odds = 1 (equal chance of success and failure), and log(1) = 0 for any base.
Q3: Yes, the log(odds) values are symmetric: P = 0.10 gives −2.197 and P = 0.90 gives +2.197 (mirror images). This symmetry means a model can treat “more likely” and “less likely” on equal footing — impossible with raw odds (0.111 vs. 9.000 are not symmetric around any meaningful center).

title: Teacher’s Notes: Video Quiz — Foundations of Logistic Regression (Q1–Q23)
text:
2.4 Q9 — The Logit Function


Code
p_respond <- mean(mail_data$responded == "yes")
cat("Response rate:", round(p_respond, 4), "\n")


Response rate: 0.0473 


Code
log_odds_respond <- log(p_respond / (1 - p_respond))
cat("Log(odds)    :", round(log_odds_respond, 3), "\n")


Log(odds)    : -3.002 


Expected answers:

Response rate: ≈ 0.0473
Log(odds): ≈ −3.004

Q3 code:


Code
# Convert .pred_yes to log(odds)
preds |>
  mutate(log_odds = log(.pred_yes / (1 - .pred_yes))) |>
  select(customer_id, .pred_yes, log_odds) |>
  head()



  



What to look for: Student correctly identifies plogis() as the inverse of log(p/(1-p)) — i.e., plogis(log_odds) gets back the probability.

title: Teacher’s Notes: Video Quiz — Foundations of Logistic Regression (Q1–Q23)
section: 2.5 Q10 — Log(Odds) and the Normal Distribution
text:
2.5 Q10 — Log(Odds) and the Normal Distribution
Written question — no code required.
Model answer Q1: Probabilities are bounded between 0 and 1, which is inconvenient for a linear model (which can predict any value). Log(odds) transforms probabilities onto the real line (−∞ to +∞), where the assumption of normality is more plausible. Logistic regression models the log(odds) as a linear function of the predictors — so normality of log(odds) supports that linear modeling assumption.
Model answer Q2: Working in probability space, the relationship between predictors and the outcome is inherently nonlinear (the S-curve). In log(odds) space, the same relationship is linear — which is what the linear predictor \(\beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \ldots\) in logistic regression actually models. Linear models are far simpler to estimate and interpret.
What to look for: Students should connect log(odds) to the linear predictor, not just mention “normality” in the abstract.

title: Teacher’s Notes: Video Quiz — Foundations of Logistic Regression (Q1–Q23)
section: 3.1 Q11 — What Is an Odds Ratio?
text:
3.1 Q11 — What Is an Odds Ratio?


Code
tier_summary <- mail_data |>
  group_by(loyalty_tier) |>
  summarise(
    yes = sum(responded == "yes"),
    no = sum(responded == "no"),
    odds = yes / no
  )

tier_summary



  



Code
gold_odds <- tier_summary |> filter(loyalty_tier == "Gold") |> pull(odds)
bronze_odds <- tier_summary |> filter(loyalty_tier == "Bronze") |> pull(odds)

OR <- gold_odds / bronze_odds
cat("Gold odds  :", round(gold_odds, 4), "\n")


Gold odds  : 0.05 


Code
cat("Bronze odds:", round(bronze_odds, 4), "\n")


Bronze odds: 0.0507 


Code
cat("Odds Ratio :", round(OR, 3), "\n")


Odds Ratio : 0.985 


Expected answers (may vary slightly by seed run):

Gold odds: ≈ 0.055
Bronze odds: ≈ 0.044
OR (Gold/Bronze): ≈ 1.25

Q4: An OR > 1 means Gold tier customers have higher odds of responding than Bronze. Whether this is “stronger” or “weaker” depends on the other tiers — students should compare across all four in Q13.
Common mistake: Computing Bronze/Gold (reverse direction) — OR < 1 is also valid but should be flagged as “reversed.”

title: Teacher’s Notes: Video Quiz — Foundations of Logistic Regression (Q1–Q23)
text:
3.2 Q12 — Asymmetry and Log(Odds Ratio)


Code
log(OR)


[1] -0.01486402


Code
OR_reverse <- bronze_odds / gold_odds
log(OR_reverse)


[1] 0.01486402


Expected answers:

log(OR): ≈ +0.22
log(OR_reverse): ≈ −0.22

Q3: The two values are equal in magnitude but opposite in sign — perfect symmetry around 0. This is the key property that makes log(OR) useful: “no relationship” = 0, “positive” = above 0, “negative” = below 0, and reversing the direction just flips the sign rather than changing from 0.11 to 9 (raw odds ratio range).

title: Teacher’s Notes: Video Quiz — Foundations of Logistic Regression (Q1–Q23)
text:
3.3 Q13 — Effect Size Interpretation


Code
tier_summary |>
  mutate(
    OR_vs_bronze = odds / bronze_odds,
    log_OR = log(OR_vs_bronze)
  ) |>
  arrange(desc(abs(log_OR)))



  



What to look for:

Platinum tier typically shows the largest |log(OR)| — highest response odds
Bronze is the reference (OR = 1, log(OR) = 0 by construction)
Student identifies the tier with the largest absolute log(OR) as the “strongest” predictor
Plain English example: “Platinum customers are X times as likely to respond as Bronze customers” — students should compute exp(log_OR) to get the multiplier for the business communication

title: Teacher’s Notes: Video Quiz — Foundations of Logistic Regression (Q1–Q23)
text:
3.4 Q14 — Fisher’s Exact Test


Code
gold_bronze <- mail_data |>
  filter(loyalty_tier %in% c("Gold", "Bronze"))

ct <- table(gold_bronze$loyalty_tier, gold_bronze$responded)
ct


        
          yes   no
  Bronze   61 1202
  Gold     27  540


Code
fisher.test(ct)



    Fisher's Exact Test for Count Data

data:  ct
p-value = 1
alternative hypothesis: true odds ratio is not equal to 1
95 percent confidence interval:
 0.6272765 1.6808508
sample estimates:
odds ratio 
  1.014967 


What to look for:

p-value will typically be > 0.05 for Gold vs. Bronze specifically — because loyalty_tier was generated independently of responded in mail_data (no relationship was built in), so students should conclude: not statistically significant
Q4 written: Fisher’s Exact Test is preferred for small samples (< ~5 expected counts in any cell), because chi-square relies on a normal approximation that breaks down with sparse tables.







ImportantTeaching Point



This is a deliberate result — the dataset was generated without any relationship between loyalty_tier and responded. Students who conclude “significant” have likely misread the p-value. This is a good moment to discuss the difference between descriptive odds ratios (which can differ from 1 by chance) and inferential tests.

title: Teacher’s Notes: Video Quiz — Foundations of Logistic Regression (Q1–Q23)
text:
3.5 Q15 — Chi-Square Test


Code
chisq.test(ct)



    Pearson's Chi-squared test with Yates' continuity correction

data:  ct
X-squared = 1.1897e-28, df = 1, p-value = 1


What to look for:

p-value should be similar to Fisher’s (typically both > 0.05)
Conclusions should agree — if they don’t, this is worth discussing
Q3 written: With 3,000 rows and reasonable cell counts, chi-square is appropriate here. Fisher’s becomes essential when any expected cell count falls below 5.

title: Teacher’s Notes: Video Quiz — Foundations of Logistic Regression (Q1–Q23)
text:
3.6 Q16 — Wald Test Concept
Written question — no code required.
Model answer Q1: “Many standard deviations from zero” means the log(OR) is large relative to its uncertainty (standard error). If the log(OR) is 2 standard deviations from zero, there is strong evidence the true relationship is not zero — similar to a z-test.
Model answer Q2: Lasso’s penalty drives small coefficients to exactly zero — effectively the same goal as a Wald test identifying “useless” predictors. Both are asking “is this coefficient meaningfully different from zero?” — Lasso uses a regularization penalty rather than a formal hypothesis test, but the outcome (zeroing out weak predictors) is similar.
Model answer Q3: The video says to use the test most commonly accepted in your specific field or context — there is no universal winner.

title: Teacher’s Notes: Video Quiz — Foundations of Logistic Regression (Q1–Q23)
section: 3.7 Q17 — Connecting Odds Ratios to the Course
text:
3.7 Q17 — Connecting Odds Ratios to the Course
Written question — no code required.
Model answer Q1: A positive coefficient for freq_12mo means that as purchase frequency increases by one unit (after normalization), the log(odds) of responding increases. Exponentiating the coefficient gives the odds ratio — e.g., if the coefficient is 0.3, then exp(0.3) ≈ 1.35 means each additional purchase-frequency unit multiplies the odds of responding by 1.35.
Model answer Q2: After normalization, all predictors are in standard deviation units. A coefficient of 0.3 for freq_12mo and 0.2 for income means frequency has a larger effect per standard deviation, regardless of whether frequency was measured in counts and income in dollars. Without normalization, coefficients would be in incompatible original units and cannot be meaningfully compared.

title: Teacher’s Notes: Video Quiz — Foundations of Logistic Regression (Q1–Q23)
text:
4.1 Q18 — Linear vs. Logistic Regression
Written question — no code required.
Model answer Q1: Linear regression predicts a continuous outcome (e.g., order value). Logistic regression predicts the probability of a binary outcome (e.g., responded yes/no).
Model answer Q2: A straight line is unbounded — it can predict values below 0 or above 1, which are impossible for a probability. At the extremes of predictor values, the line gives meaningless probability estimates. The logistic curve is bounded (0, 1) by design.
Model answer Q3: For a very frequent, high-income customer, a linear model might predict a probability of 1.3 or higher — a value that cannot be interpreted as a probability. This makes it impossible to use the output for targeting decisions that require a valid probability.

title: Teacher’s Notes: Video Quiz — Foundations of Logistic Regression (Q1–Q23)
text:
4.2 Q19 — The S-Shaped Logistic Curve


Code
plogis(-3)


[1] 0.04742587


Code
plogis(0)


[1] 0.5


Code
plogis(3)


[1] 0.9525741


Expected answers:

plogis(-3) ≈ 0.047 — very low probability (customer very unlikely to respond)
plogis(0) = 0.500 — exactly 50/50
plogis(3) ≈ 0.953 — very high probability

Q2: plogis(0) = 0.5 because: \(P = \frac{1}{1+e^0} = \frac{1}{1+1} = 0.5\). When the linear predictor is 0, there is no information pushing toward yes or no, so the probability defaults to 0.5.
Q3 (the data generator line):
prob = plogis(-3 + 0.02*(age-45) + 0.3*log(income/50000) + 0.1*freq_12mo - 0.005*recency_days)
The linear predictor says: response probability is driven by being older than average (+age), having higher income (+log income), purchasing more frequently (+freq_12mo), and having purchased recently (−recency_days). The intercept of −3 sets a low baseline probability (~4.7%), reflecting a realistic direct-mail response rate.

title: Teacher’s Notes: Video Quiz — Foundations of Logistic Regression (Q1–Q23)
section: 4.3 Q20 — Maximum Likelihood vs. Least Squares
text:
4.3 Q20 — Maximum Likelihood vs. Least Squares
Written question — no code required.
Model answer Q1: With a binary outcome (0 or 1), the “residual” (predicted − actual) is either the probability itself or 1 minus the probability — not a continuous error that can be squared and summed in a meaningful way. Minimizing squared 0/1 residuals does not correspond to finding the best-fit curve.
Model answer Q2: Maximum likelihood finds the curve that assigns the highest combined probability to the responses that were actually observed — the model that best “explains” the data as seen.
Model answer Q3: Without residuals, we use classification metrics (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, ROC AUC from Week 3) and the log-likelihood / deviance for assessing overall fit. These replace R² and RMSE that linear regression uses.

title: Teacher’s Notes: Video Quiz — Foundations of Logistic Regression (Q1–Q23)
text:
4.4 Q21 — Predicting Probability vs. Class


Code
# Distribution of predicted probabilities
ggplot(preds, aes(x = .pred_yes, fill = responded)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 40, alpha = 0.7, position = "identity") +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of Predicted Probabilities",
    x = "P(responded = yes)",
    y = "Count"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()









Code
# Typical predicted probability
preds |> summarise(median_pred = median(.pred_yes), mean_pred = mean(.pred_yes))



  



What to look for:

Most predicted probabilities cluster well below 0.5 (typically < 0.15), because the model has learned the true 4.7% response rate
The 0.5 threshold therefore classifies almost everyone as “no” — achieving high accuracy (≈ 95%) but essentially zero sensitivity
Better threshold: something like 0.05–0.10 would catch more responders at the cost of more false positives — the exact tradeoff is what the ROC curve (Week 3) is designed to explore
Metric from Week 3: roc_auc or examining sensitivity/specificity trade-off at different thresholds

title: Teacher’s Notes: Video Quiz — Foundations of Logistic Regression (Q1–Q23)
section: 4.5 Q22 — Wald’s Test and Variable Selection
text:
4.5 Q22 — Wald’s Test and Variable Selection


Code
coef_tbl <- mail_fit |>
  extract_fit_parsnip() |>
  tidy() |>
  filter(term != "(Intercept)") |>
  arrange(desc(abs(estimate)))

coef_tbl



  



What to look for:

Several predictors will have estimate = 0 — zeroed out by Lasso
freq_12mo should have a positive coefficient (more purchases → higher response probability) — this matches the data generating process which included +0.1 * freq_12mo
recency_days should have a negative coefficient (more days since last purchase → lower response probability)

Q1 written: Both Lasso and the Wald test aim to identify predictors that contribute no meaningful signal. Wald test does so via hypothesis testing (is the coefficient significantly different from zero?). Lasso does so by penalizing coefficient magnitude until weak predictors collapse to exactly zero. Both produce sparser, more interpretable models.
Q3 interpretation example: “A positive coefficient for freq_12mo means that customers who purchase more frequently have higher odds of responding to the campaign — each standard deviation increase in purchase frequency multiplies the odds of responding by exp(coefficient).”

title: Teacher’s Notes: Video Quiz — Foundations of Logistic Regression (Q1–Q23)
section: 4.6 Q23 — Synthesis: From Logs to Logistic Regression
text:
4.6 Q23 — Synthesis: From Logs to Logistic Regression
Written question — no code required.
Model answer:
The four videos form a chain where each concept solves a problem created by the previous one:

Logs → Odds: Odds are asymmetric (0 to 1 vs. 1 to ∞). Taking the log of odds creates a symmetric scale around zero, where 0 means “no association.”
Odds → Odds Ratios: We compare odds across groups as a ratio rather than a difference, because multiplicative changes are natural for proportions (a 2× increase in odds is meaningful regardless of whether the base odds are 0.01 or 0.5; an additive difference of 0.01 means very different things).
Odds Ratios → Logistic Regression: The logit (log odds) is exactly what the linear predictor in logistic regression models: \(\log\left(\frac{P}{1-P}\right) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \ldots + \beta_k x_k\). The coefficients are log(odds ratios) — the same concept from Step 1-3, now estimated from data instead of computed from a contingency table.

Q2 one-sentence answer: “.pred_yes = 0.08 means the model estimates there is an 8% chance this specific customer will respond to the mailing, based on their combination of age, income, purchase frequency, recency, channel, region, and loyalty tier — after converting the linear predictor through the sigmoid function.”

About These Slides
This slide deck gives you a big-picture overview of Module 2 before you work through the full codebook. Think of it as the map, not the territory — 27 slides covering the five stops of the module at a conceptual level, with just enough code to orient you before you see the full implementation.

title: M2 - Data Splitting (Ch5) & Preprocessing with recipes (Ch8)
text:
Before We Open the Codebook




Today’s notebook is long. This is the map, not the territory.


Five stops: the data, the split, the recipe, the model, the inspection.


Hold onto the big ideas — the syntax will make more sense once you see why each step exists.


Set expectations: this deck is short on purpose. The codebook has the full depth; this is orientation so students aren’t lost in syntax before they understand the shape of the problem.

title: M2 - Data Splitting (Ch5) & Preprocessing with recipes (Ch8)
text:
Today’s Five Stops




The dataset — a direct-mail marketing campaign
The split — why we set data aside before touching it
The recipe — a reusable preprocessing pipeline
The workflow — bundling recipe + model so nothing gets forgotten
Inspecting — prep(), bake(), and reading what the recipe learned

title: M2 - Data Splitting (Ch5) & Preprocessing with recipes (Ch8)
text:
Meet the Prospects




3,000 simulated customers. One question: will they respond to a mailing?




age, incomedemographics


recency_days, freq_12mobehavior — RFM style


channel, region, loyalty_tiercategorical context


respondedyes / no — what we predict

title: M2 - Data Splitting (Ch5) & Preprocessing with recipes (Ch8)
section: Two Problems, Hiding in Plain Sight
text:
Two Problems, Hiding in Plain Sight





Right-skewed variables
income and avg_order_amt — a few big spenders pull the tail out long.



Missing values
~8% of income, ~5% of avg_order_amt are NA — realistic, not a bug.


Both are completely ordinary in marketing data. Both need to be handled before modeling — that’s the whole point of today.

title: M2 - Data Splitting (Ch5) & Preprocessing with recipes (Ch8)
text:
Why Log Income?




Median income ≈ $49K. Mean income ≈ $59K. They disagree because of the long right tail.




Raw scalelong tail, hard for models to weight fairly


log(income)roughly symmetric, easier to model




We’ll see the before/after histograms live in the codebook — this is why step_log() exists.

title: M2 - Data Splitting (Ch5) & Preprocessing with recipes (Ch8)
text:
The Golden Rule




Fit everything on training data.


Apply it to test data.


Never the reverse — that’s data leakage, and it makes performance look better than it really is.

title: M2 - Data Splitting (Ch5) & Preprocessing with recipes (Ch8)
text:
Why the Test Set Exists




The test set stands in for future prospects the model hasn’t met yet.


Any number we compute from the full dataset — a mean, a median, an imputed value — quietly leaks test-set information into training.


That leak is invisible in your code, but it shows up later as a model that performs worse in the real world than it did in your notebook.

title: M2 - Data Splitting (Ch5) & Preprocessing with recipes (Ch8)
text:
Stratified Splitting



initial_split(mail_data, prop = 0.80, strata = responded)



80% trainingrecipe + model learn here


20% testtouched once, at the very end




Direct-mail response rates run 1–5%. Without strata = responded, a small test set could end up with almost no responders by chance.

title: M2 - Data Splitting (Ch5) & Preprocessing with recipes (Ch8)
M2 - Data Splitting (Ch5) & Preprocessing with recipes (Ch8)
text:
Six Steps, One Pipeline





1Set rolecustomer_id is an ID, not a predictor


2Imputefill missing income / avg_order_amt with the median


3Log transformtame right-skewed dollar variables


4Normalizecenter + scale every numeric predictor


5Dummy encodeturn categories into 0/1 columns


6Zero-variance filtersafety net — drop predictors with no signal

title: M2 - Data Splitting (Ch5) & Preprocessing with recipes (Ch8)
text:
Step 2 — Impute: Median, Not Mean



step_impute_median(all_numeric_predictors())

The same right-skew that makes us log-transform income is why the median beats the mean here — the mean gets dragged toward outliers, the median doesn’t.

title: M2 - Data Splitting (Ch5) & Preprocessing with recipes (Ch8)
text:
Step 3 — Log Transform



step_log(income, avg_order_amt, recency_days, 
         base = 10, offset = 1)

offset = 1 exists for one reason: log(0) is undefined. Adding 1 first sidesteps that.

title: M2 - Data Splitting (Ch5) & Preprocessing with recipes (Ch8)
text:
Step 4 — Normalize



step_normalize(all_numeric_predictors())

Centers and scales every numeric column to the same unit-free range.


Required for distance-based models (KNN, SVM). Strongly recommended for regularized logistic regression — coming up next.

title: M2 - Data Splitting (Ch5) & Preprocessing with recipes (Ch8)
text:
Step 5 — Dummy Encode



step_dummy(all_nominal_predictors())

channel, region, loyalty_tier become 0/1 columns — k − 1 per factor, to avoid redundancy between columns.

title: M2 - Data Splitting (Ch5) & Preprocessing with recipes (Ch8)
section: The Golden Rule, Applied to Recipes
text:
The Golden Rule, Applied to Recipes



mail_prep <- prep(mail_rec)              # learn from training
train_baked <- bake(mail_prep, new_data = NULL)
test_baked  <- bake(mail_prep, new_data = mail_test)

prep() learns the medians, means, and factor levels — once, from training data. bake() applies those learned values anywhere, including to the test set.


Re-running prep() on the test set separately would re-estimate everything from test data — exactly the leakage we just spent two slides avoiding.

title: M2 - Data Splitting (Ch5) & Preprocessing with recipes (Ch8)
M2 - Data Splitting (Ch5) & Preprocessing with recipes (Ch8)
text:
A Quick Word on Regularization




With many predictors, ordinary logistic regression can overfit — memorizing noise instead of signal.


Regularization adds a penalty that discourages large coefficients, forcing a simpler, more generalizable model.




Ridgeshrinks all coefficients, keeps every predictor


Lassocan zero out predictors — built-in selection


Elastic Netblends both, often the safe default




Full mathematical detail (the penalty formulas, mixture parameter table) lives in the codebook callout — this slide is just enough for students to recognize the three names and roughly what each does before they see the equations.

title: M2 - Data Splitting (Ch5) & Preprocessing with recipes (Ch8)
text:
Today We Fix the Knobs



logistic_reg(penalty = 0.01, mixture = 1) |>  # Lasso
  set_engine("glmnet") |>
  set_mode("classification")

mixture = 1 → pure Lasso. penalty = 0.01 is a reasonable starting value — later in the course we’ll tune it properly with tune() and cross-validation.

title: M2 - Data Splitting (Ch5) & Preprocessing with recipes (Ch8)
section: Don’t Just Trust It — Look Inside
text:
Don’t Just Trust It — Look Inside



tidy(mail_prep)                    # what did each step do?
tidy(mail_prep, number = 3)        # zoom into one step

Every prep()-ed recipe can be inspected. If a model behaves strangely later, this is where you check whether preprocessing did what you expected.

title: M2 - Data Splitting (Ch5) & Preprocessing with recipes (Ch8)
text:
What You’ll Actually Type Today





1initial_split()split, stratified by response


2recipe() + step_*()declare the preprocessing plan


3prep() + bake()learn from train, apply everywhere


4workflow()bundle recipe + model


5fit() + augment()train, then predict on new data

title: M2 - Data Splitting (Ch5) & Preprocessing with recipes (Ch8)
text:
Where This Goes Next




Week 3 — once a model is fit, how do we know if it’s actually any good? Metrics and resampling.


Week 4–5 — new model types, same recipe-and-workflow scaffolding you’re about to type today.


Everything from here on reuses this pattern: split → recipe → workflow → fit. Today is the only day you build it from scratch.

title: M2 - Data Splitting (Ch5) & Preprocessing with recipes (Ch8)
text:
Let’s Open the Codebook




Five stops.


One reusable pattern.

title: Week 2 Assignments: Logarithms in R
text:
Assignments
   
  
  Overview
  1 Setup
  2 Assignment L.1 — Dollar to Log
  3 Assignment L.2 — Log Back to Dollar (Median and Mean)
  4 Assignment L.3 — Geometric Standard Deviation
  5 Assignment L.4 — Confirm with Real Data
  6 Assignment L.5 — Base 10 vs. Natural Log
  7 Summary
  


Overview
These assignments practice the logarithm operations covered in the Week 2 notebook (Section 2.1 — Explaining how Income distribution changes from normal to logged data).
You will practice:

Converting dollar values to log scale (log(), log10())
Converting log-scale values back to dollars (exp())
Computing the geometric standard deviation (GSD)
Working with the real mail_data dataset
Understanding why the choice of log base doesn’t affect modeling


Reference: Week 2 notebook, Section 2.1 — https://jaejungca.github.io/recipes/w02_ds_preprocessing.html




1 Setup
Run this chunk first — it loads the packages and recreates the direct-mail dataset used throughout the course.


Code
library(tidyverse)

# ── Recreate the direct-mail dataset ─────────────────────────────────────────
set.seed(123)
n <- 3000

mail_data <- tibble(
  customer_id = paste0("C", str_pad(1:n, 5, pad = "0")),
  age = round(rnorm(n, mean = 45, sd = 12)),
  income = round(rlnorm(n, meanlog = 10.8, sdlog = 0.6)),
  recency_days = round(rexp(n, rate = 1 / 60)),
  freq_12mo = rpois(n, lambda = 3),
  avg_order_amt = round(rlnorm(n, meanlog = 4.2, sdlog = 0.5), 2),
  channel = sample(
    c("email", "direct_mail", "digital"),
    n,
    replace = TRUE,
    prob = c(0.5, 0.3, 0.2)
  ),
  region = sample(
    c("West", "South", "Midwest", "Northeast"),
    n,
    replace = TRUE
  ),
  loyalty_tier = sample(
    c("Bronze", "Silver", "Gold", "Platinum"),
    n,
    replace = TRUE,
    prob = c(0.4, 0.3, 0.2, 0.1)
  ),
  responded = rbinom(
    n,
    1,
    prob = plogis(
      -3 +
        0.02 * (age - 45) +
        0.3 * log(income / 50000) +
        0.1 * freq_12mo -
        0.005 * recency_days
    )
  )
) |>
  mutate(
    income = if_else(runif(n) < 0.08, NA_real_, income),
    avg_order_amt = if_else(runif(n) < 0.05, NA_real_, avg_order_amt),
    responded = factor(responded, levels = c(1, 0), labels = c("yes", "no"))
  )

glimpse(mail_data)


Rows: 3,000
Columns: 10
$ customer_id   <chr> "C00001", "C00002", "C00003", "C00004", "C00005", "C0000…
$ age           <dbl> 38, 42, 64, 46, 47, 66, 51, 30, 37, 40, 60, 49, 50, 46, …
$ income        <dbl> 44793, 40269, 20560, 32261, 233070, 47933, 84804, 43883,…
$ recency_days  <dbl> 103, 102, 27, 115, 47, 106, 4, 62, 13, 21, 139, 100, 9, …
$ freq_12mo     <int> 4, 3, 0, 3, 1, 3, 5, 2, 1, 6, 1, 3, 3, 3, 6, 1, 1, 1, 6,…
$ avg_order_amt <dbl> 80.19, NA, 54.80, 46.49, 64.86, 78.43, 39.45, NA, 68.26,…
$ channel       <chr> "email", "digital", "digital", "email", "digital", "dire…
$ region        <chr> "West", "South", "Northeast", "Northeast", "Midwest", "W…
$ loyalty_tier  <chr> "Bronze", "Bronze", "Bronze", "Bronze", "Gold", "Silver"…
$ responded     <fct> no, no, yes, no, yes, yes, no, no, no, no, no, no, no, n…





2 Assignment L.1 — Dollar to Log
Customer C00005 has an income of $233,070.

Convert this value to the natural log: log(233070)
Convert this value to log base 10: log10(233070)
Verify both results using R
In your own words, why does the recipe use base = 10 rather than the natural log?







TipHint



In R, log(x) computes the natural log (\(\ln x\)) and log10(x) computes log base 10. You can also write log(x, base = 10) — this is exactly what step_log(..., base = 10) uses internally.




Code
# Your code here





3 Assignment L.2 — Log Back to Dollar (Median and Mean)
The income variable was generated with meanlog = 10.8 and sdlog = 0.6. These parameters describe the distribution of log(income), not income itself.

Convert the log-scale median (10.8) back to dollars using exp()
Convert the log-scale mean (10.98) back to dollars using exp()
Verify these match the formulas from the Week 2 notebook:

\[\text{Median} = e^{\mu} = e^{10.8}\]
\[\text{Mean} = e^{\mu + \sigma^2/2} = e^{10.8 + 0.6^2/2}\]

Why is the mean higher than the median on the dollar scale?







TipHint



exp(x) is the inverse of log(x). If log(income) = 10.8, then exp(10.8) gives you income back in dollars. For the mean, compute 10.8 + 0.6^2 / 2 first, then apply exp().




Code
# Your code here





4 Assignment L.3 — Geometric Standard Deviation
The geometric standard deviation (GSD) describes the typical spread of income around the median on the original dollar scale.
\[\text{GSD} = e^{\sigma} = e^{0.6}\]

Compute the GSD using exp(0.6)
Using the median income from L.2 (~$49,021), compute the typical range:
